PROJECT 1

The Global Launch

Secure, Globally Distributed Static Portfolio Hosting on AWS

Technical Implementation Documentation - Final Architecture

Internship Deliverable - DecodeLabs Cloud Computing (AWS/Azure) 
Tools: AWS S3, CloudFront, IAM, ACM, Route 53, GitHub Actions (OIDC) Date:00/07/2026 

Author: Kazeem Jimoh 

Live URL: https://static-portfolio-site.qossim005.online 


  1. Project Objective

As a freelance developer, the brief was to deliver a personal portfolio website for a client who needed global availability, fast load times worldwide, and no ongoing server management overhead. 

This documentation covers the full implementation as actually built, including the base requirement (public static hosting on object storage) and the additional infrastructure layered on top of it in response to the complete project rubric: global content delivery, account-level security hardening, automated deployment, and a custom secured domain. 

1.1 Rubric Requirements 

 

Requirement 

Delivered 

Public storage bucket / hosting 

Yes - Amazon S3 

index.html and 404.html uploaded 

Yes 

Static Website Hosting / public read configuration 

Yes (superseded by CloudFront + OAC, see Section 3) 

Publicly accessible URL 

Yes - custom HTTPS domain 

Global low-latency access 

Yes - Amazon CloudFront 

Secured perimeter (IAM roles, not root keys) 

Yes - MFA root, scoped IAM, OIDC federation 

CI/CD ready workflow 

Yes - GitHub Actions, OIDC auth 
  1. Final Architecture

The final architecture is deliberately layered so that no single component is a single point of security failure: the storage layer is private, the content delivery layer is the only path to it, and the deployment credentials are temporary rather than static. 

  1. Implementation Walkthrough

STEP 1: Base S3 Bucket, Hosting, and Public Policy

Created bucket static-portfolio-site-560205084952 in us-east-1. Uploaded index.html and (later) 404.html. Enabled Static Website Hosting (index document: index.html) and attached a public-read bucket policy scoped to s3:GetObject only. This satisfied the baseline rubric requirement and produced the first working (HTTP-only) endpoint. 

This configuration was later superseded (not deleted) by the CloudFront/OAC setup in Step 3, once the global low- latency and security requirements were addressed. 

STEP 2: AWS Account Security Hardening

The account was initially being operated as the root user for all tasks - flagged as a direct conflict with the rubric's 'secured perimeter' requirement. Remediated as follows: 

●        Enabled MFA on the root user via an authenticator app. 

●        Confirmed and removed any root-level access keys - root is now console-login-only, MFA-protected, and reserved for account-level tasks only. 

●        Created a dedicated IAM user (portfolio-admin) for day-to-day console work, with permissions attached via a group rather than directly to the user. 

STEP 3: CloudFront + Origin Access Control (Private Bucket)

A CloudFront distribution was created with the S3 bucket's REST endpoint (not the website endpoint) as its origin, using a newly created Origin Access Control (OAC). This was a deliberate choice over the simpler option of pointing CloudFront at the existing public website endpoint, because OAC allows the bucket to be locked down to fully private - removing the direct public attack surface entirely. 

Configuration: 

 

Setting 

Value 

Origin 

static-portfolio-site-560205084952.s3.us-east- 1.amazonaws.com 

Origin access 

Origin Access Control (OAC), SigV4 signing 

Viewer protocol policy 

Redirect HTTP to HTTPS 

Default root object 

index.html 

Origin path 

(blank) 

The bucket policy was replaced to trust only this specific CloudFront distribution (via an AWS:SourceArn condition), and 'Block all Public Access' was re-enabled on the bucket. The public-read statement from Step 1 was removed entirely. 

Custom error handling for 404.html was configured at the CloudFront layer (Error Pages: HTTP 403 from origin → custom response /404.html, HTTP 404), since S3's own website-hosting error-document behavior no longer applies once traffic is routed through the REST endpoint. 


STEP 4: CI/CD via GitHub Actions (OIDC)

Rather than storing a static AWS access key as a GitHub secret, deployment authentication uses GitHub's OIDC identity federation with AWS IAM. AWS issues no long-lived credential to GitHub; instead, each workflow run requests a short-lived, auto-expiring token by assuming a dedicated IAM role. 

●        An OIDC identity provider for token.actions.githubusercontent.com was configured in IAM (account-wide; shared safely across projects since trust is scoped per-role, not per-provider). 

●        A dedicated IAM role, github-actions-oidc-deploy, was created with a trust policy restricted to this specific GitHub repository. 

●        The role's permission policy is scoped narrowly: s3:PutObject / DeleteObject / ListBucket on this bucket only, and cloudfront:CreateInvalidation on this distribution only - no read access, no other resources. 

Workflow behavior on every push to main: 

●        Checks out the repository 

●        Assumes the deploy role via OIDC (no stored secret) 

●        Runs aws s3 sync with --delete, mirroring the repo into the bucket exactly (additions and deletions both propagate) 

●        Runs aws cloudfront create-invalidation on /* so viewers see the new deployment immediately rather than a stale cached version 

A prior version of this pipeline used a static IAM user access key stored as a GitHub secret. It was fully replaced with the OIDC role above; the access key was deleted from AWS once the OIDC path was confirmed working, so no long-lived deploy credential remains anywhere. 

STEP 5: Custom Domain with TLS (Route 53 + ACM)

The client-facing URL uses a subdomain of an existing domain, secured with a certificate issued by AWS Certificate Manager, rather than the default *.cloudfront.net address. 

 

Setting 

Value 

Domain registrar 

Namecheap 

DNS provider 

Amazon Route 53 (nameservers delegated from Namecheap) 

Subdomain 

static-portfolio-site.qossim005.online 

Certificate 

AWS Certificate Manager, DNS validated, issued in us- east-1 (required region for CloudFront) 

DNS record 

Route 53 Alias (A) record → CloudFront distribution 

The certificate was requested and DNS-validated directly through Route 53 (using ACM's native 'create records in Route 53' integration), then attached to the CloudFront distribution as its custom SSL certificate alongside the matching Alternate Domain Name. The final DNS record was created using CloudFront's integrated 'Route domains to CloudFront' flow, which provisions the Route 53 alias record automatically and avoids manual record-type mismatches. 

  1. Debugging Log

The following issues were encountered and resolved during implementation. They are included here deliberately: each one reflects a genuine gap between 'clicked the right buttons' and 'understood why the system behaved that way,' and diagnosing them is a core part of the skill this project was meant to build. 

  1.   CloudFront WAF Permission Error

Error: wafv2:CreateWebACL not authorized. Cause: the CloudFront console's 'Enable security protections' option auto-provisions an AWS WAF Web ACL, requiring permissions beyond the IAM user's original S3-only scope. Resolution: the IAM user's permission set was incrementally expanded to include CloudFront/WAF actions needed to complete distribution setup, rather than defaulting to a broad administrator policy - preserving least-privilege intent while unblocking the task. 


  1.   S3 AccessDenied on the CloudFront URL

After the distribution was created and the bucket policy updated, the site still returned a raw S3 AccessDenied XML response. Root caused to two separate misconfigurations, found in sequence: 

●        Default Root Object was unset on the distribution, so a request for '/' had no mapping to index.html. 

●        Origin Path was incorrectly set to '/index.html', which caused CloudFront to request '/index.html/index.html' from S3 - a nonexistent key. Clearing the Origin Path field resolved this. 

Because the bucket policy grants GetObject only (no ListBucket), S3 returns a generic AccessDenied for missing keys rather than a descriptive 404 - this made the underlying cause harder to identify from the error message alone and required checking each layer of the request path individually. 

  1.   OIDC AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity Not Authorized

GitHub Actions failed to assume the deploy role. Root cause: the IAM role's trust policy 'sub' condition contained a stray space in the repository name (qezman/ static-portfolio-site), which does not match GitHub's actual token claim. Corrected the condition to a clean, wildcarded match (repo:qezman/static-portfolio-site:*), which also made the trust policy less brittle against branch-name changes. 

  1.   CloudFront Certificate / Alternate Domain Name Mismatch

CloudFront rejected the alternate domain name with a certificate-coverage error. Root cause was an exact-string mismatch between the certificate's domain name and the alternate domain name field - resolved by re-confirming both values character-for-character and re-issuing/re-selecting against the correct subdomain. 

  1.   Cross-Project Resource Collisions (Same AWS Account)

Because a separate, unrelated project shares this AWS account, two account-wide resources required care rather than blind recreation: the GitHub OIDC identity provider (only one instance is permitted per account, so it was reused and a new, separately-scoped IAM role was created for this project's repository) and DNS delegation (verified via nameserver lookup that this subdomain's records needed to be managed in Route 53, not Namecheap's own DNS, before attempting any record changes). 

  1. Skills Demonstrated

●        S3 object storage provisioning, static hosting configuration, and bucket policy authoring (public and OAC- scoped) 

●        IAM account hardening: root MFA, credential lifecycle management, least-privilege user and role scoping 

●        CDN architecture: CloudFront distribution configuration, Origin Access Control, custom error handling 

●        DNS and TLS: Route 53 hosted zones and alias records, ACM certificate issuance and validation, nameserver delegation 

●        CI/CD: GitHub Actions workflow authoring, OIDC federated authentication as a replacement for static credentials 

●        Systematic debugging across a multi-layer request path (DNS → CDN → origin → storage), including correctly interpreting ambiguous error responses (e.g. generic AccessDenied) by isolating each layer independently 

●        Operating safely within a shared AWS account: identifying account-wide vs. project-scoped resources before making changes 

  1. Outcome

The project deliverable was achieved and extended beyond the base rubric: a live, globally distributed, HTTPS- secured portfolio website, served from a private object storage origin with no direct public access, deployed via a credential-less automated pipeline. 

Live URL: https://static-portfolio-site.qossim005.online 

  1. Conclusion

This project began as a minimal exercise in static hosting on object storage and evolved, in direct response to the full project rubric, into a production-representative architecture: private storage behind a CDN, hardened account access, a credential-less deployment pipeline, and a properly issued TLS certificate on a custom domain. The debugging encountered along the way - particularly the layered AccessDenied troubleshooting and the OIDC trust policy issue - reflects the kind of diagnostic reasoning that distinguishes configuring cloud infrastructure from actually understanding it.
